excerpt: The exoteric ranks of the daʿwa mirror the spiritual hierarchy of the cosmos, each visible dignity corresponding to a hidden one in the world of ibdāʿ.
source_location_hint: On the correspondence of the daʿwa ranks with the spiritual world (Mashraʿ on the ḥudūd)

excerpt: The choicest of the human race were brought forth from the supports of the Originator and stand to the rest of mankind as the ruby to common stone.
source_location_hint: On the emanation of the noblest human substances from the supports of ibdāʿ

excerpt: By contemplation of himself and the order of the heavens, the first Excellent Person came to deny lordship of all that exists and to affirm it of the Originator alone.
source_location_hint: On the natural attainment of tawḥīd through inward contemplation

excerpt: The ulū al-ʿilm whom Allāh joins with Himself and the angels as witnesses to tawḥīd are the ḥudūd of the daʿwa, established by knowledge upon justice.
source_location_hint: Taʾwīl of Q 3:18 — the ulū al-ʿilm as the spiritual ḥudūd

excerpt: The Bāb stands to the Imam as the First Emanator stands to the First Intellect — the inbiʿāth proceeding from ibdāʿ.
source_location_hint: On the relation of the Imam and Bāb mirrored in the spiritual ḥudūd

excerpt: The Paradise of the faithful is a station beyond the grasp of sense and conception, named only by intimation.
source_location_hint: On the eschatological abode beyond sensory description

excerpt: The cycles of disclosure proceed through ordered cores and bodies until the final assembly is gathered at the House of God.
source_location_hint: On the periodisation of cycles (adwār) and the final body

excerpt: The barzakh is the interval in which the souls of the departed find their seat until the day of their raising.
source_location_hint: On the barzakh and the ascent of the soul to its horizon

excerpt: The architecture of the dawr al-kashf — in which prophetic millennia accumulate toward the gathering of the Qāʾim — is developed at length in Fatimid cosmological literature, where the supernal intellects (al-ʿuqūl) and their attendant horizons frame the unfolding of sacred time.
source_authority: Kirmānī's Rāḥat al-ʿAql is the most systematic Fatimid statement of the hierarchy of intellects (the ʿuqūl) and their relationship to the unfolding of prophetic cycles, situating the eschatological gathering within a cosmological architecture.
source_location_hint: throughout, esp. discussions of the supernal hierarchies and the relation of intellects to prophetic cycles

excerpt: The people of the upper three levels of each station are called the jadd, the fatḥ, and the khayāl of that station. They are the intermediaries between the recipient and his Lord… At every level, this triadic hierarchy is at work.
source_authority: Sijistānī's Kitāb al-Yanābīʿ is a foundational Fatimid statement on the spiritual hierarchies (ḥudūd) and the structure of revelatory mediation, treating the modes by which the divine word descends to the prophets and Imāms through ranked intermediaries.
source_location_hint: wellsprings concerning the hierarchies and the modes of inspiration

excerpt: In the world of the visible ḥijāb, Muḥammad — who is the veil of the Mīm — received his support from his three earthly intermediaries: ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib, Abū Ṭālib, and ʿAbdullāh. In the language of the sharīʿa, these three intermediaries correspond to Jibrāʾīl, Mīkāʾīl, and Isrāfīl.
source_authority: Kirmānī correlates the spiritual ḥudūd of the Fatimid daʿwa hierarchy with the angelic intermediaries of revelation, establishing the structural parallel by which physical kin of the Prophet figure their spiritual counterparts.
source_location_hint: sections treating the correspondence of the daʿwa hierarchy with the angelic ranks

excerpt: Maulātnā Khadīja accepted Islām on a Monday, and ʿAlī accepted Islām on a Tuesday, with a span of five prayers between Khadīja's Islām and ʿAlī's.
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān's Taʾwīl al-Daʿāʾim is the canonical Fatimid work of esoteric exegesis (taʾwīl) applied to the practices and personages of the early community, articulating the typological reading by which Khadīja figures Maryam and ʿAlī figures ʿĪsā.
source_location_hint: sections on the early community and the typology of the Prophet's household

excerpt: Fāṭimah is part of me; whoever pleases her pleases me, and whoever angers her angers me. She is the mistress of the women of the worlds.
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, traditions on the virtues of the Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: The rational soul possesses several faculties stationed in the ventricles of the brain — the cogitative (mufakkira), the imaginative (mutakhayyila), and the retentive (ḥāfiẓa) — through which it apprehends, composes, and preserves the forms of knowledge.
source_authority: Fatimid dāʿī, chief intellectual authority of the Iraqi school (d. ca. 411/1020)
source_location_hint: Discussions of the human soul and its cognitive faculties

excerpt: The washing of the face in wuḍūʾ stands for the acceptance of the knowledge of the Nāṭiq, while the wiping of a portion of the head signifies the partial knowledge that the believer attains of the ranks above him.
source_location_hint: Book of ritual purification, esoteric exegesis of wuḍūʾ

excerpt: The Qāʾim is so named because he stands forth (yaqūm) with the gathering of his cycle, and the Resurrection (qiyāma) is the unveiling of the inner realities at the close of an age, when the partial resurrections are subsumed into the universal.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ-sections on the cycles of prophecy and the offices of the Qāʾim

excerpt: The great cycle is composed of cycles of revelation (kashf) and cycles of concealment (satr) in alternation; each is closed by a Qāʾim who gathers up the assemblies of his cycle, and from these gatherings the cosmic ledger of souls is constituted.
source_authority: early Fatimid dāʿī of the Yemeni school (d. ca. 346/957)
source_location_hint: Treatises on the cycles of sacred history

excerpt: The Tenth Intellect (al-ʿAql al-ʿĀshir) is the proximate originator of the world of generation, and the perfected souls who complete the ascent attain their seat in its horizon, where they assist in the configuration of the coming cycle.
source_authority: Fatimid dāʿī, chief intellectual authority of the Iraqi school (d. ca. 411/1020)
source_location_hint: Sections on the descent of souls, the Tenth Intellect, and the eschatological ascent

excerpt: The cycle of each nāṭiq is closed by seven Imams, the seventh of whom rises to inaugurate the cycle of the next speaking prophet; thus the chain of nuṭaqāʾ from Adam to Muḥammad and the Qāʾim is held together by the sevenfold Imamate.
source_location_hint: Seventh Mushāriʿ on the prophetic cycles and the ranks of the daʿwa

excerpt: After Muḥammad b. Ismāʿīl the Imams entered concealment (satr) and the daʿwa was carried on by the abwāb who openly bore witness to the hidden Imam; these mustawdaʿūn together compose the interval that prepares the manifestation of the Mahdī.
source_location_hint: Section on the satr and the abwāb of the concealed Imams

excerpt: The seventh Qāʾim of a great cycle gathers into himself not only the labour of his own period but the totality of the six preceding qāʾims, so that the entire dawr is summed in his person as in a single locus of light.
source_location_hint: Discourse on the Qāʾim and the consummation of the cycles

excerpt: The Tenth Intellect (al-ʿAql al-ʿĀshir) is the horizon upon which souls that have attained kamāl awwal stand as partial vicegerents, ranged by precedence of arrival rather than by intrinsic difference.
source_location_hint: Mushāriʿ on the Tenth Intellect and the ascent of perfected souls

excerpt: The vicegerency of ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib is the inner axis around which the cycles of revelation revolve; when one cycle has run its course, his authority descends upon whomever is established at the inauguration of the next.
source_location_hint: Section on the walāya of ʿAlī and the transmission of authority through the dawrs

excerpt: No rank in the hierarchy is raised until it has appointed within itself a successor of its own measure; thus the Imam appoints the Imam, and the Qāʾim appoints the seventh of the seventh as the caliph of the whole.
source_location_hint: Majlis on the transmission of the Imamate and the conditions of elevation in rank

excerpt: In the unveiled age the virtues of each nāṭiq are sent forth in succession: first the saints of Muḥammad, then those of Jesus, then of Moses, of Abraham, of Noah, and of Adam, each appearing again across a span of seven thousand years until the great cycle is filled.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the cycles of prophethood and the reappearance of the virtues of the nuṭaqāʾ

excerpt: The letter nūn, opened at its two ends, becomes the image of the alternation of revelation and concealment: between two great periods of fifty thousand years stands the dawr of satr, which is itself the womb of the next manifestation.
source_location_hint: Discourse on letters and the symbolism of nūn within the cosmic cycles

excerpt: Letters are the visible signs of intelligible realities; in nūn the believer reads the curving descent of revelation back into concealment, and the rising again of concealment into a fresh disclosure.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the symbolism of the disjoined letters of the Qurʾān

excerpt: The haykal of the Qāʾim is composed of the members of the daʿwa as the body of a man is composed of his limbs; in each member a particular ḥadd is articulated, and the analogy must be read according to the haykal that is intended.
source_location_hint: Section on the haykal of the Imam and the correspondence of the ḥudūd to bodily members

excerpt: Within the long age of kashf the believers do not remain at one pitch; toward its close there comes a period of languor in which the forms of revelation are mixed with the forms of fitra, and the people are drawn toward music and the arts.
source_location_hint: Discourse on the divisions of the kashf and the decline of zeal at the end of the cycle

excerpt: On the verse 'Surely the former and the latter shall be gathered together for a known time' the awaited Qāʾim alone holds the dignity of joining the first and the last in a single congregation, for he is the ʿayn that gathers all that has been distributed across the cycles.
source_location_hint: Majlis on Q 56:49 and the gathering of the awwalūn and ākhirūn at the Qāʾim's hour

excerpt: The Mubdiʿ, exalted is He, is removed from every attribute by which the originated is qualified; thus the Originator and the originated cannot be designated by a single name without a distinction of intent in the utterance.
source_location_hint: on the doctrine of tawhid and the distinction between al-Mubdiʿ and al-mubdaʿ al-awwal

excerpt: From Ibrahim proceeded two lines: the line of Ishaq, which bore the manifest prophetic ministry up to ʿIsa, and the line of Ismaʿil, which bore the concealed imamate until it issued forth in Muhammad and his asas.
source_location_hint: on the bifurcation of the prophetic and imamatic lines through the sons of Ibrahim

excerpt: Among the Imams there is the mustaqarr, settled in his rank, and the mustawdaʿ, who carries the deposit until its term is fulfilled; the deposit then returns to the established line.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-walaya, on the categories of the Imams

excerpt: The Messenger of God said: 'I and ʿAli were one light before God created Adam; then the light was divided, and one half was commanded to be Muhammad and the other half to be ʿAli.'
source_location_hint: on the unity of the light of the Prophet and the wasi

excerpt: Beneath the Imam stands the Bab, who is the gate through which the seeker reaches the light of the Imam; he is, as it were, the heart of the temple of the Imam's daʿwa, and beneath him stand the hujaj and the duʿat in their ranks.
source_location_hint: on the hierarchy of the ḥudud of the daʿwa

excerpt: On the day of Ghadir Khumm the Messenger completed the religion: he raised the hand of ʿAli and proclaimed, 'Whoever I am his mawla, this ʿAli is his mawla' — and on that day the imamate stood forth as the very pillar of the faith.
source_location_hint: on the designation of ʿAli at Ghadir Khumm

excerpt: The natiq and the asas are two lights from a single source; each veils the other in the world of disclosure, so that the outward priority of the Prophet and the outward silence of ʿAli are, in their inward truth, two faces of one rank.
source_location_hint: on the relation of the natiq and the asas

excerpt: The nāṭiqs of the cycles—Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muḥammad—each open a new dawr of revelation, and each successor's law abrogates and completes what preceded; the inheritance of tanzīl, taʾwīl, and the ḥaqāʾiq accumulates across the cycles.
source_location_hint: discussion of the six nāṭiqs and the prophetic cycles

excerpt: The terrestrial daʿwa is ordered by ten hudūd corresponding to the celestial intellects: Nāṭiq, Asās, Imam, Bāb, Ḥujja, Dāʿī, Maʾdhūn, Mukāsir, Muʾmin, and Mustajīb.
source_location_hint: section on the hudūd of the daʿwa and their correspondence to the celestial intellects

excerpt: Moses's petition that his breast be opened, and the granting of Aaron as his vizier and waṣī, is the tanzīlī foreshadowing of the sharḥ al-ṣadr granted to the Prophet and the appointment of ʿAlī as his asās; in each cycle, the nāṭiq is paired with his asās.
source_location_hint: taʾwīl of the narrative of Moses and Aaron

excerpt: The wilāya of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is the perfecting pillar by which Islam is completed; the Prophet declared at Ghadīr Khumm, 'Whoever I am the mawlā of, ʿAlī is his mawlā.'
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya

excerpt: Al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn are imāmān, whether they rise or whether they remain seated; their father is better than both of them—a saying transmitted from the Prophet concerning the imamate of his two grandsons.
source_location_hint: chapter on the imams of the Family of the Prophet

excerpt: O God, bless Muḥammad and the Family of Muḥammad, the best of Your creation, the trustees of Your revelation, and the most select of Your servants.
source_location_hint: opening invocations on the Family of the Prophet

excerpt: The believer's ascent passes through every ḥadd of the spiritual hierarchy without halting at any single rank, for the goal is union with the highest limit through continuous traversal.
source_location_hint: yanbuʿ on hierarchical limits (ḥudūd) of the spiritual ascent

excerpt: The seventh Imām of each cycle gathers into himself the six who precede him and inaugurates the disclosure proper to his period.
source_authority: early Fatimid-era Ismaili daʿi (d. ca. mid-4th/10th c.)
source_location_hint: sections on the rank of the seventh Imām and the cycles of the imāmate

excerpt: Muḥammad b. Ismāʿīl is the inaugurator of the cycle of disclosure who unveils the inner meaning of the Sharīʿa brought by Muḥammad Muṣṭafā.
source_location_hint: discussions of Muḥammad b. Ismāʿīl as qāʾim and ṣāḥib al-kashf

excerpt: The First Intellect (al-ʿAql al-Awwal), being the first emanation of the Originator, encompasses in its knowledge all that has been and all that shall be, since nothing of being lies outside its embrace.
source_location_hint: yanbuʿ on al-ʿAql al-Awwal and its universal scope

excerpt: The two seas in Sūrat al-Raḥmān are interpreted esoterically as the streams of prophecy and imāmate, whose conjoining is the function of the unique mediating ḥadd.
source_location_hint: chapters interpreting Quranic similitudes of pairs and conjoinings

excerpt: No act of worship, prophethood, or witness is accepted before God except through the wilāya of ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib, for he is the gate of acceptance for every preceding messenger.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-walaya, opening chapters on the indispensability of the wilāya

excerpt: ʿAlī bore the burden of struggle on the Prophet's behalf in every battle, and through his suffering he discharged the recompense owed to all preceding prophets who had served their Imāms.
source_authority: chief daʿi (dāʿī al-duʿāt) of the Fatimid daʿwa (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: majālis on the rank of ʿAlī and his services to the Prophet

excerpt: The believer's longing for the knowledge of the Imam is not extinguished by death but is continually nourished from the Imam as the source of spiritual sustenance.
source_location_hint: On the believer's eternal nourishment by the Imam's knowledge — a recurring theme across the Majālis

excerpt: Each Imam constitutes a gathering of the spiritual forms received from his predecessor, and in turn prepares such a gathering for his successor — the unbroken transmission of the Lāhūt being the very condition of the daʿwa's continuity.
source_location_hint: On the transmission of esoteric knowledge through the lineage of the Imams

excerpt: The cosmic Kursī designates the seat upon which the order of the celestial hierarchy is unfolded, encompassing the ranks of the spirituals (the heavens) and their dependents (the earth).
source_authority: Fatimid dāʿī and chief metaphysician (d. after 411/1020)
source_location_hint: On the cosmic hierarchy and the symbolic referents of Quranic cosmological terms

excerpt: The Ismaili taʾwīl tradition reads the numerical equivalences embedded in the divine names and the names of the awliyāʾ as evidentiary of the structural unity between the names of God and the persons of the Imams.
source_location_hint: On the symbolic and numerical correspondences of divine names with the Imamic hierarchy

excerpt: The shorter sūras and salient verses of the Qurʾān admit a taʾwīl in which each corresponds to a particular ḥadd within the cosmic and historical hierarchy of the daʿwa.
source_location_hint: On the esoteric correspondences between Quranic units and the ranks of the daʿwa

excerpt: Al-Qadi al-Nu'man treats the testaments of the prophets — Ibrahim's above all — as paradigms of transmission, in which the prophet hands on to his successors not merely a creed but a constituted community held in spiritual trust, so that 'dying in submission' (la tamutunna illa wa-antum muslimun) signifies the completion of this consignment.
source_location_hint: section on the stories of Ibrahim and Ya'qub

excerpt: Nasir-i Khusraw distinguishes the outward Islam, accessible through profession of the Word, from iman as its interior reality, and locates above iman a further, concentrated submission — the surrender of one's form to the rightful holder of the hudud — which alone perfects the soul's ascent.
source_location_hint: chapters on the ranks of Islam, iman, and the hudud

excerpt: Al-Mu'ayyad expounds Laylat al-Qadr as the night on which the spiritual hudud of the da'wa receive and convey the inner light from the Imam down through the ordered ranks, so that the 'salam' of the sura (salamun hiya hatta matla' al-fajr) names the very chain of transmission rather than a mere stillness.
source_location_hint: majlis treating Surat al-Qadr
